desc:
Onward through a deep cleft in the mountains, marches the pass. Sheer cliffs
stand to either side, leaving only the narrowest of routes through which to
travel, and the trail itself is almost lost amongst fallen rocks and debris. To
the east the path begins a sharp descent, whilst bending southward it can only
climb into the heights of the mountains.
name: The Redhorn Pass

desc:
The mountain face to the west is far too sheer for any traveller to climb, even
ignoring the added difficulty of snow and ice most of the year. The Redhorn Pass
ascends to the north, and climbs steeply upward to the south. Eastward a sheer
drop-off makes an uninviting prospect. Looking up the steep slopes, a glimpse of
the red rock peak can be seen which gives the mountain and its pass their names.
name: The Redhorn Pass

desc:
Many bushes of varying shapes and sizes are scattered around and between the
coniferous trees. Even this far from the peak of the Caradhras it is much too
cold for any deciduous trees or less-toughened underbrush. The path rises up
from the north and continues west.
name: The Redhorn Pass

desc:
Oops, your grip on the cliff wall has loosened and you are unable to hold on any
longer! As you fall to your death shards of rocks from the killer cliff impale
themselves into your body. Your senses and all feeling go numb from the
excruciating pain as you come into contact, full force with the cliff floor.
Well, you certainly have reached the bottom, perhaps not from the most favoured
route, but nonetheless you are at the bottom. Better luck next time!
name: At the Bottom of the Cliff

desc:
Oops, your grip on the cliff wall has loosened and you are unable to hold on any
longer! As you fall to your death shards of rocks from the killer cliff impale
themselves into your body. Your senses and all feeling go numb from the
excruciating pain as you come into contact, full force with the cliff floor.
Well, you certainly have reached the bottom, perhaps not from the most favoured
route, but nonetheless you are at the bottom. Better luck next time!
name: At the Bottom of the Cliff

desc:
A cliff is here just off the side of the pass. It is almost vertical in nature
and the rock formations which jut out from it possess a razor sharp quality. To
reach the bottom of the cliff you must climb down the face of the mountain. The
cliff itself curves from west to north here, so that the drop-off lies both
south and east of you.
name: A Sheer Cliff

desc:
A steep and treacherous trail is carved into the mountain. It is extremely
narrow, barely accommodating two people side by side. To the side is a sheer
cliff which drops hundreds of feet. The mountains seem to almost touch the sky
as you shift your glance upwards.
name: The Redhorn Pass

desc:
Emerging from a deep cleft between two soaring ridges, the trail now seems to
cling rather precariously to the mountainside. To the south you might stray a
short distance from the path, but to the west is a precipitous drop- off. The
path itself descends to the east.
name: The Redhorn Pass

desc:
This trail is the only way in which this mountain range can be crossed.
Northwards you can see the peak of the cruel and mighty Caradhras, to the south
you note another great mountain, the Celebdil. From this point in the pass you
can either follow it to the east or to the west.
name: The Redhorn Pass

desc:
A small cave has been hewn into the rock here. The odour of the animals is
getting even stronger, and its origin seems to be the cagedoor in the south
wall. To the west, a tunnel mouth opens and goes on into the darkness.
name: A Kennel

desc:
With its low ceiling and bare rock walls this is not a pleasant place. The cell
continues to the north, and to the west there is a heavy-looking oaken door. A
few bundles of rugs lie on the ground, obviously used for sleeping.
name: A Dark Cell

desc:
You are standing in a natural burrow, surrounded by grey stone walls. The only
visible exit is to the west, back to the low twisting tunnel. The cavern must be
used as a storage room, as you notice some shelves by the walls. The ground is
uneven, covered by a filthy rug.
name: A Burrow

desc:
You are standing on a wooden ladder, descending into the darkness below on a
stairwell which has been hewn into the grey rock of the mountain. Above, you
barely notice a trapdoor in the ceiling and north of here, occasional strange
snarling can be heard.
name: A Stairway

desc:
The narrow tunnel comes from the south and abruptly ends at the heavy metal grid
in the west wall and you notice a heavy lock hanging through the metal loops.
The smell of animals is filling the air and its origin seems to be coming from
this direction.
name: A Tunnel to the Pit

desc:
You have found out the origin of the growls you heard. It's a dark sullen cave,
with the deep claw marks all over the stone walls. Above you can be seen a trap
covering the opening, while to the east you notice a metal grid. There are also
quite a lot of separate bones lying strewn about the cave floor.
name: A Smelly Pit

desc:
The trail seems to fade out into the mountain side as it comes downslope from
the west. South, the ground dips down into a small space of rock below an
overhange visible even from here. The many tall trees are more abundant here
than farther up the mountain, and look to continue to grow more numerous to the
south.
name: Mountain Trail

desc:
A tall outcropping of rock forms the western wall of this place; the slope down
remains unclimbable. Only to the south and north would you be able to get up out
of this bit of sheltered rock. Although they can be seen in the distance in
almost all directions, there is an absence of trees here. Small tufts of rough
grass show in the short summer, but at all other times no life seems to live
here.
name: Mountain Trail

desc:
Nestled between tall mountain ridges, stony ledges and rocky overhangs, this
extremely narrow trail would be almost invisible even from directly above, as it
makes its way from a wider gully to the west into a craggy and inhospitable area
to the east.
name: On a Hidden Trail

desc:
To the south of you the trail begins to emerge from its hidden route, as it
slowly makes its ascent toward the top of the ridgeline. To the east the
mountainside ends in a dizzying drop into a snow-filled valley sheltered between
two mountains.
name: On a Hidden Trail

desc:
The serpentine ridgeline continues writhing and curving through the mountains.
The path is treacherous as no foothold is safe, and if you trust your eyes, what
appears to be firm may send you plunging to your death.
name: A Mountain Ridgeline

desc:
Eastwards, the white lace of a series of waterfalls can be seen tumbling down
into a rich dale. A deep, shadowy forest runs the length of the valley, skirting
the eastern banks of an oval mere. To the west lies a shallow cave, whose rear
is barely concealed in shadows.
name: A Broad Shelf

desc:
The fine, gravelly scree provides no traction whatsoever. Almost immediately,
the rocks give way, allowing no safe footing. Southwards the slope is less
severe and the grade of rock is larger. Additonally, it is possible to travel
upwards by means of a stone ladder onto a broad shelf.
name: Severe Scree Slope

desc:
An unsteady pile of moraine rests here threatening any who might try to scale
its side. Deep grooves in the scree slope to the west demonstrate the path that
these rocks must have taken. To the east can be seen a talus slope, descending
fast into the dale.
name: Rockfall

desc:
A long and wide slope of talus connects this ridge to the dale below. The
terrain here is rocky with an occasional hint of the black soil that is
particular to the lowlands of this area. To the northeast a large slab of rock
extends from the ridge creating a stone shelf above you, but due west of you the
land falls off dangerously toward a severe rockfall.
name: Sloping Ridge

desc:
Directly across the dale the steep slopes of Celebdil rear up above you, their
craggy faces hemming in the valley below. From here, a precarious talus slope
stretches eastward, leading toward the distant mere. The ridge continues its
ascent to the north, but diminishes as it runs south and west.
name: A Jagged Ridge

desc:
Broken dolomite and scree compose the ground of this narrow ridge. Small spindly
heather attempts to grow in the shattered rock but its brown hues betray its
failure. Far to the southwest can be seen a dark, oval mere, which lies amidst a
rich dale, stretching from southeast to northwest.
name: A Jagged Ridge

desc:
A sheer granite incline stands westwards preventing any lateral movement along
the ridge, but a rectangular cut in the rock allows a limited access onto the
precarious rock spire above. Eastwards the Redhorn pass crosses the ridge and
makes a descent into the Dale below.
name: Narrow Chimney

desc:
Standing amidst the austere mountains of Moria, this narrow spire allows a wide
and sweeping view of the surrounding areas. Northwards can be seen the Redhorn
Pass as it creeps around Caradhras and climbs into the Dimrill Dale in the
south. Far to the south, Moria's eastern gate can be seen emerging from the rock
face.
name: A Granite Spire

desc:
Shards of scree and broken rock cover this precarious ascent. Occasionally,
small rocks will fall lazily down the slope gathering at the bottom. A sheer
cliff looming over the slope prevents any further travel to the west but it is
possible to move laterally along the ridge by going north.
name: Scree Slope

desc:
The bottom of this steep-sided rocky hollow has become filled by masses of
jagged stones of all sizes. Even a few boulders have tumbled here from the
craggy mountain-sides above. With near vertical ascents to the east and west,
the only ways out of this hollow are to scale its rather gentler northern slope,
or to continue south, in the hope of finding an alternative egress.
name: Precipitous Gathering of Moraine

desc:
The cross-section of the cave is like a squashed circle. The floor is fairly
flat, whilst the walls and roof describe a low, sweeping curve. The cold, hard
stone is scarred by numerous tiny cracks and fissures, and through these quite a
considerable quantity of water seeps, and it is at this end of the cave that it
has pooled, running down the very slight incline of the ground.
name: In a Low-Roofed Cave

desc:
The cave is at its widest here, narrowing to little more than a tunnel at either
extremity. The roof, however, remains fairly low above your head. Tiny rivulets
of water seep though innumerable narrow cracks in the walls. The water then
gathers, tricking across the slightly sloped floor of the cave to form a pool at
one end.
name: Deep in a Low-Roofed Cave

desc:
A wide, round hole punctures the low roof of the cave, and immediately beneath
this stands a large pile of rocks, boulders and rubble, which must have fallen
into the cavern to make the aperture. The stone of the walls and of the ceiling
offers a clue as to how the hole was made, for they are almost covered in a
network of cracks and fissues, suggesting an inherent instability in the
groundrock of this immediate area.
name: In a Low-Roofed Cave

desc:
Ringed about by steep cliffs and mountain peaks is a small, secluded meadow. The
land rises steeply from its centre toward the rock faces, leaving only a
comparatively small area which is flat enough to support vegetation, but this
area is surprisingly rich. Tiny wildflowers spring up amid thin grass, and even
a few small bushes and shrubs grow here. Not far from one of the walls of the
basin a large round hole lets down into a cavern below.
name: In a Secluded Mountain Meadow

desc:
Golden, brown alpine grass and purple flowered heather grow amidst dolemite
protrusions as the rocky soil prohibits any more plant life. Westwards, the
ground slopes down toward a scree slope, whilst to the north the ridge continues
its ascent across the flanks of Celebdil. To the south the slope falls abruptly
into a shadowy valley far below.
name: Sloping Ridge

desc:
Giant slabs of talus litter the ground making footing uneasy. A mild slope runs
westward up onto the scree sides of Celebdil. The scent of rich earth and
wildflowers drifts from the east, where a low ridge rises, dropping sharply to
the south into a shadowy dale.
name: A Rocky Climb

desc:
Weatherworn, lichen-coated slabs of talus lie scattered on the banks of this
dale. Running down from the diminishing western ridge, this slope comes to an
end as it gently meanders to the bottom of the valley. A few wildflowers grow
here in the cracks filled with soil.
name: Talus Covered Slope

desc:
The wide, multi-coloured rocks lay scattered on the ground, the product of a
recent slide off of Celebdil. Glimpses of brown, dead grass can be seen under
the stone revealing that this was once a grassy field. Several large clawed
footprints are amidst, all seemingly gathered near a particularly large stone.
name: Talus Field

desc:
The thick scent of damp earth and decay is overwhelming. Scattered bones lie
across the floor, stripped of their meat and bleached with age. Large, clawed
footprints design the muddy floor and reveal this to be the dwelling of some
large animal.
name: Muddy Burrow

desc:
The footing upon this narrow ridge is decidedly unsteady. Wind and rain, ice and
snow have all taken their toll on the rocks, and repeated seasons of freezing
and thawing have led to immense cracks, with massive slabs of stone shearing off
the ridge and smaller debris creating extensive scree fields in the area. To the
northwest the ridge becomes completely impassable, but to the south it appears
rather safe.
name: Atop a Narrow Ridge

desc:
Low and rocky, this saddle point in the ridgeline nevertheless supports some
stunted shubs and a few wildflowers, perhaps due to its sheltered location
between several higher areas. A trail once passed along this ridge, climbing
from the north, but it peters out as the land rises east of here.
name: Atop a Low Ridge

desc:
You find yourself upon a strange old trail, which travels a winding path through
the mountain crags, climbing steadily toward the south. It is in a terrible
state, with cracks leading from the ground up the surrounding walls, and the
floor itself is littered with rocks and stones.
name: At the End of the Strange Trail

desc:
The road begins a steep descent into the dale below. Broken rock and pavement
litter the path, making travel precarious. To the east are many sparkling
cascades flowing into a dark mere. The northern direction reveals only the
endless sky of mountains, occasionally peaked with a glittering glacier. The
thick scent of earth becomes prominent as the road breaks down into a muddy,
rocky path to the south.
name: The Redhorn Pass

desc:
This ancient, once paved road has turned into a steep muddy path from years of
negligence. Water seeping down from the Dimrill Stair and Naragul destroyed this
particular section. Westwards begins a rocky ascent onto the side of Celebdil.
To the south is a rich, grassy field littered with wildflowers and rocks. The
trail makes a bend here to the east, then runs parallel to long tarn.
name: A Muddy Path

desc:
Sharp rock fragments lie scattered across the road. Purple flowered heather
lines the roadside, the only thing growing in this stony turf. Eastwards lies a
deep, long, tarn. Its midnight waters reflect the shining peak of Celebdil to
the west. The path follows the tarn to the south or continues northwards onto a
muddy trail.
name: A Rocky Path

desc:
Stunted birches and scraggy pines attempt to grow in between the rocks along the
roadside. This broken road reveals that is was once a well-travelled path by the
scattered slabs of pavement and its wide nature. It continues into the mountains
to the north, and through the dale to the south.
name: A Rocky Path

desc:
Dwarven works of stone lay ruined along the roadside. Moss and lichen flourish
on these decaying statues. The path continues up into the mountains north, and
runs out of the dale to the south. To the east you can see a dark mere.
name: A Rocky Path

desc:
Here the track which borders the smooth, dark lake to the northeast is met by a
stone road descending from the south. There is ruined dwarven stone-work all
around. Dark, sad pines border the track. Westwards, the grassy field meets the
base of Celebdil.
name: A Rocky Path

desc:
Tracing a path not far from the long, oval lake, the narrow trail makes an
westward bend here, drawing closer to the water. Not far from the roadside, at
the bottom of a long, grassy slope, a tall standing stone can be seen, just by
the shore of the Mirrormere.
name: A Path by a Standing Stone

desc:
Climbing downward, between the arms of the dale, the path now makes its way past
the end of the Mirrormere, and bends to the east, away from Silvertine, and
round the base of Cloudyhead. Some distance to the east you can make out the
bright sound of a lively stream.
name: The Trail out of the Valley

desc:
The ledge here has been broken in many places. Great pieces of rock are missing
at various points of the walkway making it a treacherous climb. With great care
and difficulty the ledge can still be compromised but its condition is far from
safe. One must tread with care!
name: On a Broken Ledge

desc:
This is a deep ravine, and as you peer from side to side only tall steep slopes
meet your gaze. As you plod on along the bottom the sounds of footsteps are
echoed through the ravine. You can follow it to the north or to the east.
name: A Deep Ravine

desc:
A deep and winding ravine is here in the mountains. Both sides of the ravine are
extremely steep and unclimable. The only roadworthy route is along the bottom of
the ravine, it goes north and south.
name: A Deep Ravine

desc:
Steep walls overshadow you to the south, the east and the west, whilst to the
north this scree-filled hollow stretches out before you. The ground beneath your
feet is extremely treacherous, since the rocks and stones seem to shift and
slide with your every movement. Yet even here there is a little vegetation. Tiny
weeds poke their heads tenaciously through the rocky floor, a mute testament to
the faceless will of life to find a way to survive.
name: Precipitous Gathering of Moraine

desc:
Bright pansies and purple flowered gentians provide variation to the wide,
grassy field. Westwards, the terrain becomes rockier as a talus slide has
covered the ground. A rough path, littered with broken pavement, runs north and
south at the far side of the meadow, beside the shore of the lake.
name: A Grassy Field

desc:
Various fragrances of wild flowers, and the rich scent of the black soil in
which they flourish, dominates the air. Carmine orchids and bright, purple
gentians dot the flowing meadow as it continues to the south and west. Not far
to the east a path marks the route through the valley, and just beyond it lie
the shores of the Mirrormere.
name: A Wild Meadow

desc:
Moria's eastern gate stands proudly at the top of this aged, marble stair.
Emerging from the jagged rock face its fine sculpture and strong columns hint at
the former Dwarven kingdom's majesty. The gleaming glacier capped mountains of
Celebdil, Fanuidhol, and Caradhras dominate the sky line. Northwards, cradled
between two jagged ridges, lies a shadowy dale. At its northern point can be
seen a dark, oval mere. To the east the mountains level out into a rolling
forest.
name: Threshold of Moria

desc:
A lofty peak looms high above you to the south, quite unscalable from this
point. However, by following the land as it rises to the west you can attain the
top of a long ridgeline, which cuts across the countryside from northwest to
southeast, and which appears to climb toward summit. By descending the more
gentle slope to the east you would find yourself at the southern end of the
Mirrormere.
name: On the Slopes of a Rising Ridge

desc:
Though not one of the highest peaks in the Misty Mountains, this mountaintop
nevertheless affords a spectacular view of the surrounding lands. To the north-
west stand mighty Celebdil and Caradhras and to the northeast Fanuidhol is the
dominant feature. Due east, and far below you, lies the vast expanse of the
Golden Wood, spanning the Silverlode, and reaching far into the distance.
name: Atop a Lofty Peak

desc:
Near vertical in places, this section of the ridgeline is extremely steep and
treacherous. Although eastward the terrain becomes easier to travel, to the
north it becomes steeper still, demanding that anyone who wishes to journey from
here to the mountain peak resort to climbing.
name: Cresting a Steep Ridge

desc:
Cracks and fissures criss-cross the walls and floor of this cavern, making a
pattern that looks like nothing so much as giant spider webs. But the webs which
are strung across the chamber itself are no illusion. A noisome smell pervades
the place, and the remains of many creatures, some small, and others not so
small, litter the ground.
name: A Smelly Cavern

desc:
Climbing higher here than it did further to the east, the ridgeline also
describes a curve to the northwest, reaching steadily toward a mountain peak.
Far below you, at the bottom of a vertical cliff, you can see a narrow valley,
which makes a steady ascent into the mountains to the west. And east of here the
ridge stretches out before you.
name: On a Narrow Mountain Ridge

desc:
From the southeast to the northwest the craggy ridge makes its way steadily
towards a lofty pinnacle. Far below you lie the southern reaches of the Dimrill
Dale, and in the distance the shimmering waters of the Mirrormere dominate the
view. From here you can trek down the ridgeline to the south, or you can climb
higher along it to the west.
name: Climbing a Narrow Ridge

desc:
High above you the mountain slopes climb toward the summit. Although this is not
one of the tallest of the Misty Mountains, it is nevertheless a formidable peak.
Its sides are sheer in many places, and the crown cannot be reached by a direct
assault from here, but only by traversing one of the prominent ridges, which cut
across the land from the northwest to the southeast.
name: On a Rocky Mountainside

desc:
The terrain here is fairly steep, and travelling across it is not made any
easier by the fact that loose rocks and stones are scattered all around, making
your footing decidedly uncertain. West and south the land climbs up toward the
mountains, but to the east and north you can make your way into the southern end
of a long valley.
name: A Rocky Scramble

desc:
The ground climbs briskly to the west, as it reaches into the mountains, but
eastward it falls away toward a vale. Between valley and peaks lies a land
littered by innumerable stones, and a few larger boulders, the products of
rockslides from above. But all around this debris green grass and myriad tiny
wildflowers flourish, despite the sloping ground.
name: A Rocky Field

desc:
Rocks and stone litter the ground all about you, but between them a thin carpet
of short, green grass grows. Here and there tiny wildflowers peep up through the
green stalks, lending new colours to the tapestry of green and grey. Descending
eastward from the mountains, this wide expanse is bordered by a rocky trail,
which itself traces its route from the path of a lively little stream.
name: Rocky Field beside a Trail

desc:
Standing north of a pleasant little valley, this ridge cuts a swath across the
land from east to west. Further north the land falls away gently toward the
southern end of the Dimrill Dale, and in the distance the landscape is dominated
by a large oval lake, which nestles in the valley floor, between the arms of
Celebdil and Fanuidhol.
name: On a Faltering Ridge

desc:
This is the beggining of a long and deep ravine. It branches away from the trail
to the north, and carries on westwards. The base of the ravine has a glazed
look, due to constant weathering by the elements. The ground is completely bare
and naked, apart from some stones scattered to the north.
name: A Deep Ravine

desc:
This is just another bend in the trail which you have been following. The trail
continues north and east. To your south there is a difference in the rock. You
are unable to see clearly just what lies in that direction from such a distance.
name: A Bend in the Trail

desc:
A collection of scree and moraine make this once grassy field lifeless and
choppy. The vertical base of Celebdil standing westwards is responsible for the
debris, as rock slides are very common in this region. To the north and east,
the ground becomes softer and the soil black, as is particular to this dale.
name: Rocky Turf

desc:
A jagged mountain ridge rises before you, its saw-toothed profile sweeping
across the land from northwest to southeast. The ground underfoot here is
treacherous with loose rocks and debris, making the ascent to the top of the
ridge that much harder. And to the north the rocky terrain continues, though the
land flattens out markedly in this direction, as it enters the floor of the
valley.
name: On the Slopes of a Rising Ridge

desc:
Though steep, this broad section of the ridgeline is fairly easily travelled. To
the southeast it climbs steadily toward a lofty mountain peak, and west of here
it descends into a saddle. To the east the land slopes off towards the valley
floor at a fairly gentle gradient.
name: Climbing a Steep Ridge

desc:
This narrow ridge rises steeply, yet steadily, from the northwest, climbing
toward a lofty mountain peak. High above the valley floor, this place offers a
grand view of the southeastern reaches of the Dimrill Dale, and in the distance
you can see the glimmering waters of a large, dark, oval lake.
name: A Steeply Rising Ridge

desc:
Here the trail continues, bending west and south at the feet of the mountain
slopes. The sky seems to hang low above the mountain tops, and the ground seems
to possess some uncanny quality you cannot define - something unnatural either
about its colour and texture, or about the shadows that fall on it.
name: A Strange Trail

desc:
The strange trail runs north-south here, following the feet of the mountains. At
this point it runs so straight and seems so long, as if it had no beginning or
end anywhere. Nothing catches your eyes on or off the trail; everything seems
barren, dull and dead.
name: On a Strange Trail

desc:
Pieces of granite protrude from the earth breaking up the otherwise
uninterrupted carpet of short green grass. Down the slope to the east is an old
track which winds around the southern tip of the cold, deep Mirrormere.
name: A Grassy Field

desc:
Bright, fragrant wildflowers dot this grassy slope into the dale. The terrain is
soft and yielding from the tiny rivulets of water, which flow down the sloping
ground, from the mountain peaks and ridges above, to join the Dimrill Stair in
its progress toward the large oval lake below.
name: A Grassy Field

desc:
The sound of rushing water from the east is all that can be heard in this barren
land. Northwards stands proud Caradhras, whose towering peak ranks tallest among
these mountains. Beside him, to the east, glitters the summit of Fanuidhol, and
to the west is Celebdil. From the turning point of this ridge, a lush dale can
be seen below.
name: The Redhorn Pass

desc:
The trail bends, making an unexpected turn. To the east your eye spies a faint
glimmer, something shiny in the distance. The Redhorn Pass continues to the
south and west, remaining as it was throughout the dark ages. This dangerous
route has stood through and survived the evil which had ravaged the land, and as
you tread upon it, memories of the past can be felt lingering in the cold, crisp
and frosty air.
name: Bend in the Pass

desc:
The small stream follows the mountain line. It travels a fairly straight path
and joins a bigger river in the east. The stream is quite strong here and seems
to be inhabited by many fish. The surface of the water flashes tinges of silver,
but perhaps it is nothing more than light playing tricks on your weary eyes.
name: The Small Stream

desc:
Travelling just north of a small, but lively stream, not too far below, the
narrow ridge undulates gently up and down as it marches steadily from east to
west. But the general trend is for the altitude to drop as you progress
eastward, toward the Dimrill Stair.
name: A Ridge by the Water

desc:
The ground here is covered with blotches of weedy and unkempt grass. The blades
are short, unable to grow fully due to the harsh conditions in this region. The
earth seems quite worn, as this is probably the only place where the local
wildlife can feed.
name: A Simple Grassland

desc:
An immensely large tundra-like plain is buried in the heart of the mountain
range. Small patches of dead grass litter the ground around you, making the the
area seem unkempt. Little other vegetation grows here, and your eye can behold
little of beauty. The tundra stretches onward to the west, growing ever more
bleak, whilst to the south there is a precipitous drop-off to the waters of a
fast-flowing mountain stream, which sweeps past to the east of here.
name: The Plains before the Water

desc:
This is the source of the river Naragul. Here it collects the droplets of water
from the snow-capped peaks and the glaciers of the Misty Mountains. It begins
very slow and narrow, but peering southwards you can see that it picks up both
speed and a wider form. The scent of grass is lingering in the air upon this
area and to the west you detect a wide plain.
name: The Naragul

desc:
A large, vicious and jagged chunk of rock extends out from the mountain. This
stone feature is sharp like a knife's edge, giving the impression that
knife-like spears are actually protruding from the mountain, spears created to
fend off the unwanted and guard the secrets which Caradhras holds.
name: A Vicious Rock Outcropping

desc:
A large, vicious and jagged chunk of rock extends out from the mountain. This
stone feature is sharp like a knife's edge, giving the impression that
knife-like spears are actually protruding from the mountain, spears created to
fend off the unwanted and guard the secrets which Caradhras holds.
name: A Vicious Rock Outcropping

desc:
A large, vicious and jagged chunk of rock extends out from the mountain. This
stone feature is sharp like a knife's edge, giving the impression that
knife-like spears are actually protruding from the mountain, spears created to
fend off the unwanted and guard the secrets which Caradhras holds. At this point
of the outcropping a rocky steppe looms above your head, providing some shelter.
name: A Vicious Rock Outcropping

desc:
This north-facing ledge is overshadowed by a rocky shelf which effectively hides
it from direct sunlight. From here, to the northwest, can be seen the great
south face of Caradhras, and beyond it the lesser peaks of the Misty Mountains
extending to the north. Due north of here the land is a clutter of stony spires
and crags.
name: Under an Overhang

desc:
This stone shelf offers a sweeping view of the golden wood of Lorien which
stretches many leagues to the east. To the southwest the mighty Silverlode can
be seen cutting through the forest, gaining momentum from the many mountain
streams and rivers feeding into it.
name: A Scenic Overlook

desc:
A strong scent of vanilla drifts through the air, emitted from one of the many
types of wildflowers this dale hosts. The black soil is soft and yielding,
allowing this meadow to flourish, but to the west the ground becomes rocky as it
nears the base of the mountains.
name: A Wild Meadow

desc:
Bright pansies and white, drooping lilies dot the otherwise uninterrupted carpet
of tall grass. The meadow is bordered by the lively waters of the Dimrill Stair
to the west, and to the south lies a long, oval lake. The field rolls on to the
north, but to the east travel is rapidly blocked by the encroaching mountains.
name: A Wild Meadow

desc:
The tarn's midnight waters reflect the icy summits of Celebdil, Caradhras, and
Fanuidhol, the mountains of Moria. The mere emits a severe cold from its
seemingly bottomless depths. To the east lies a hilly forest, whose tall
evergreens run right up onto the banks of these waters.
name: Mirrormere

desc:
The tarn's midnight waters reflect the icy summits of Celebdil, Caradhras, and
Fanuidhol, the mountains of Moria. The mere emits a severe cold from its
seemingly bottomless depths. The water hosts no fish or plant life, and lies
undisturbed by the foliage from the deep forest running eastwards.
name: Mirrormere

desc:
The tarn's midnight waters reflect the icy summits of Celebdil, Caradhras, and
Fanuidhol, the mountains of Moria. The mere emits a severe cold from its
seemingly bottomless depths. To the southeast, a tall pillar stands on a grassy
slope.
name: Mirrormere

desc:
The tarn's midnight waters reflect the icy summits of Celebdil, Caradhras, and
Fanuidhol, the mountains of Moria. The mere emits a severe cold from its
seemingly bottomless depths. To the west, a rocky path travels north - south
through the dale.
name: Mirrormere

desc:
The tarn's midnight waters reflect the icy summits of Celebdil, Caradhras, and
Fanuidhol, the mountains of Moria. The mere emits a severe cold from its
seemingly bottomless depths. Northwards the lake narrows, reaching between
mountain on the east and rocky shore to the west.
name: Mirrormere

desc:
The tarn's midnight waters reflect the icy summits of Celebdil, Caradhras, and
Fanuidhol, the mountains of Moria. The mere emits a severe cold from its
seemingly bottomless depths. Northwards the gleaming Dimrill Stair falls into
the dale. Forming a small pool at its bottom, it leaks under a pile of moraine,
slowly feeding into Mirrormere.
name: Reach of Mirrormere

desc:
Small effervescent waterfalls decorate this narrow path into the dale. The
smooth, rounded mineral rocks that bed its rapid flow, keep the stream clear. A
fine mist hangs in the air from the constant splash. Rich smells of earth emerge
from the hilly forest to the east. Westwards lies a larger fall resulting in an
icy pool. Below, the stair continues merging with a dark mere.
name: The Dimrill Stair

desc:
This tremendous dolomite protrusion offers a sweeping view of a rich and shadowy
dale. Cradled between the arms of Caradhras and Fanuidhol, it runs southwards
out of the mountains of Moria. At the head of the dale begins an oval mere,
whose dark waters reflect the rocky ridges and icy peaks of her surroundings.
Westwards can be heard the noisy Naragul as it spills into a series of small
waterfalls.
name: A Giant Crag

desc:
A large, vicious and jagged chunk of rock extends out from the mountain. This
stone feature is sharp like a knife's edge, giving the impression that
knife-like spears are actually protruding from the mountain, spears created to
fend off the unwanted and guard the secrets which Caradhras holds.
name: A Vicious Rock Outcropping

desc:
A large, vicious and jagged chunk of rock extends out from the mountain. This
stone feature is sharp like a knife's edge, giving the impression that
knife-like spears are actually protruding from the mountain, spears created to
fend off the unwanted and guard the secrets which Caradhras holds.
name: A Vicious Rock Outcropping

desc:
This small body of water is running its course through the fortress of peaks
which surround it. The water is chilly having kept most of the frigid nature of
the glaciers which it has sprung from. It has inherited the attributes of the
surrounding lands of which it is housed. It is cold and unfeeling, no warmth or
love runs through it. Much like a river of silver.
name: The Naragul

desc:
This small body of water is running its course through the fortress of peaks
which surround it. The water is chilly having kept most of the frigid nature of
the glaciers which it has sprung from. The riverbank is small and muddy, making
it extremely hard and uncomfortable to move on.
name: The Naragul

desc:
This is the meeting point of two streams, where the Naragul which originates
from small glacial pockets in the mountains absorbs the smaller stream which
flows from the west. The Naragul then continues flowing southwards where it will
eventually meet the Mirrormere. These waters which you are standing in are
flowing with quite some velocity but yet are crossable by foot.
name: The Fork of Two Rivers

desc:
This is the stream Naragul which has its roots from the great glacial peak of
the deadly Caradhras. It flows both quick and swift providing a strong current.
On the western bank you spy a trail which remains hidden by the mountains. To
the north lies a fork of the stream and southwards the Naragul flows on.
name: The Naragul

desc:
This raging torrent smashes against its jagged banks throwing up a fine mist.
The crashing of worn rocks resisting the river's barrage dominates the air.
Small eddies twirl violently above submerged rocks threatening any who might
come within their reach. To the west can be seen Celebdil, whose icy peak feeds
this brook. Southwards the Naragul begins a steep series of falls carrying into
the dale below.
name: The Naragul

desc:
The roar of the Naragul making its first drop is deafening. Falling into a small
moraine dammed pool, it flows more calmly to the east. Large rocks near the pool
are covered with a thick carpet of moss from the constant exposure to water. To
the south can be seen an aged, once paved road making a steep descent and
switchback into the dale below.
name: The Dimrill Stair

desc:
A high ridgeline rises to the west of you, and a lower mountain ridge crosses
the land to the south. To the east the land gradually flattens out, as it
approaches a region of tundra.
name: On the Slopes of a Ridgeline

desc:
The serpentine ridgeline continues writhing and curving through the mountains.
The path is treacherous as no foothold is safe, and if you trust your eyes, what
appears to be firm may send you plunging down to your death.
name: A Serpentine Ridgeline

desc:
The sheer slope climbs to the top of a reptilian-like ridge, the very backbone
of Caradhras itself. Looking up, you can see the jagged edge of the ridgeline
silhouetted sharply across the sky. Far below you, a snow-filled valley glimmers
white in a gap between two mountains.
name: A Mountain Face

desc:
A steep rockface extends from the mountain peak towards the stream which runs on
slightly lower ground. The ground is dead and barren and life is unapparent on
this mountain. Perhaps this emptiness is due to the harsh and unpleasant frost
which leeches itself to this area, or perhaps it is due to some other reason
which you cannot possibly comprehend.
name: A Mountain Face

desc:
This is the highest point of the mountain ridge - from here the dark forests of
Mirkwood can be seen near the eastern horizon, just beyond the great river
Anduin. The area surrounding the summit is extremely rough and mostly
impassable. In every direction the peaks of Hithaeglir rise, reaching towards
the sky with rocky fingers.
name: A Mountain Summit

desc:
This is a horribly steep and treacherous ridgeline. Its sides are very tall and
plunge into the depths. Above you is the mountain summit, to the east the
ridgeline continues.
name: A Mountain Ridgeline

desc:
You find yourself precariously situated on the steep southern face of the tall
stone mountain looming high overhead. The narrow, rocky ridge above looks more
stable but no less treacherous than this spot, situated about two rods above a
deep cleft surrounding a narrow trail. The slope continues in all directions.
name: A Mountain Face

desc:
This is the northern tip of the vast mountain face that spreads out like a huge
blanket covering the southern side of the mountain. To the north the face slopes
up into a very steep and narrow ridgeline.
name: A Mountain Face

desc:
A steep rockface extends from the mountain peak towards the stream which runs on
slightly lower ground. The ground is dead and barren, life is unapparent on this
mountain. Rocks of all sizes and colours are strewn all over the face of the
mountain. To the north high above you a prominent peak enshrouded in clouds is
distinguishable. To the south you can see a mountain pass far below.
name: A Mountain Face

desc:
A small stream trickles out of a crack in the rockface. It is not very deep and
is easily fordable. The water is frigid to your touch, which is not a very
pleasant sensation. The stream flows downward from the west to the east,
following the Redhorn Pass. There are many bends and curves as the stream is
obviously a new one and has not settled into the mountain yet.
name: A Small Stream

desc:
A thin rivulet of water flows smoothly alongside the Redhorn Pass. The small
current picks up and carries along the sand from the riverbed. Dark brown
pebbles line the floor, looking much like a thin layer of dirt. Damp dark moss
surrounds the rocks which are situated alongside the stream.
name: A Small Stream

desc:
A cold glacial stream flows eastward here through a deep, steep-walled valley.
The stream's rocky bed is full of stones of all sizes and colors which have
fallen from the rocky mountain faces and were washed down here in the spring
melt. There are a few evergreen bushes clinging to the valley's walls but little
other vegetation.
name: A Small Stream

desc:
This is a part of the long mountain ridge which travels adjacent to a small
stream in the south. The ridge stretches east and west, rising toward a higher
ridge in the west and getting lower to the east, where it crosses a bleak
grassland.
name: A Ridge by the Water

desc:
This mountain ridge stands on the side of a small waterway and seems like a
barrier protecting, guarding whatever secrets the stream holds. You can follow
the ridge eastwards or go down the slope to the stream.
name: A Ridge by the Water

desc:
The trail takes advantage of a rocky valley notched into the side of the
mountain as it descends to the east. A small stream fed by glacial run-off also
utilizes the valley, running parallel to the trail a short distance to the
north.
name: The Redhorn Pass

desc:
Large dolomite formations protrude from the ridge, making the area appear
jagged. Westwards one of Celebdil's mighty rock formations rears above you, and
to the east the ridge continues around the side of the bluff. Southwards a long
scree slope connects the ridge with the valley floor below.
name: A Jagged Ridge

desc:
Shattered rock and lichen-covered stones litter the floor of this rugged ridge.
To the north and west the ridge curves about the flank of Celebdil, reaching
then for the south. Broad talus slopes make a precarious descent to the floor of
the Dimrill Dale below you.
name: A Jagged Ridge

desc:
This rocky slope forms the southern side of a jagged ridge that bends from the
north to the west, descending into the hills lying far southwards. A lush
woodland stretches out to the east, terminating on the banks of a dark, oval
mere.
name: Talus Covered Slope

desc:
Wide slabs of talus lie here, rocking unsteadily, apparently the product of a
recent slide. Small flat fragments of stone slip and clatter downhill under your
feet. To the west the ground slopes up, and the slope reaches the base of
Celebdil; to the east you can see a lush, grassy field.
name: Talus Field

desc:
Giant slabs of weatherworn rocks lay unsteadily here. Rotten tree trunks and
dead pine needles under the lichen covered stones reveal that this was once a
grove of pines trees. A deep gully running down the eastern flank of Celebdil
demonstrates the path the rocks had taken. Directly above, a ornate dome
structure emerges from the rock face.
name: Talus Field

desc:
The ridge here is descending rapidly toward the Dimrill Dale, and far in the
distance, cradled by the rising mountains to either side of it is a dark, oval
mere, whose reflective waters offer a panorama of the mountains of Moria. East
and west, the ridge stretches before you, but to the south you might clamber
down the rocky slopes toward the valley floor.
name: A Jagged Ridge

desc:
A traveller heading east now would begin his descent in earnest, while the
westward bound traveller just now begins the true ascent leading to the
snowfields on the high shoulder of Caradhras. The trail runs along a small
stream flowing just to the north, sharing its narrow valley before taking to the
steep slopes.
name: The Redhorn Pass

desc:
The trail descends sharply to the east, following the floor of a shallow and
rocky valley. To the north, a shallow glacial stream dances among the rocks. To
the east, the valley seems to end, though perhaps it simply turns abruptly.
name: The Redhorn Pass

desc:
A panoramic view of the valley floor opens out before you, as the mountain ridge
tapers off at an angle in the Redhorn Pass. To the southeast you can see the
clear waters of the Mirrormere, which reflect the peaks of Fanuidhol and
Celebdil. Westward this gutted ridge of small pebbles and dirt continues.
name: Mountain Ridge

desc:
This is a broad basin full of deep, packed snow. To the south rises a winding
mountain ridge, and to the east great rocky tiers rise to an impressive peak.
Travelling on the snow is difficult, but there are no obvious signs of fissures
or crevices.
name: Snowfield

desc:
You are standing in the middle of an ancient rockslide, one which has obviously
hit the mountain with a terrible toll. It appears that the entire mountainside
has been completely destroyed and devastated. The only exit lies to the south.
name: A Collapsed Mountainside

desc:
This is a completely blocked off and devastated area, many rockslides and harsh
weather over the years have ruined the ledge. All exits are blocked off except
the one from which you entered. This part of the mountain has many sharp and
jagged edges, from where chunks of rock have been displaced. Above you the
weathered outcroppings look like the teeth of some evil creature just lurking in
the shadows waiting to devour you.
name: The Ruined Crags

desc:
The ledge continues here leading upwards, off to its side a great fall to an
unpleasant death beckons you. It may be wise to walk on the inner cutting of the
ledge. You are able to follow either upwards to a damaged area or you can head
to the south where the ledge began. One thing is for sure: you had better not
linger at this point contemplating the direction in which to continue your
journey.
name: A Rock Ledge

desc:
The shaft is almost vertical here. Were it not for what appears to be a stair-
case, it would be impossible to climb this passage, and even now the task will
not be easy, as the rock is worn quite smooth and is coated by damp moss and
algae. Twisting and turning, the tunnel descends into the stony depths of the
mountain beneath you. Not far above you the passageway ends at a wide hole,
which appears to exit only to the skies themselves.
name: At the Top of a Sloping Tunnel

desc:
Soaring high into the sky, as if attempting to reach for the clouds, this lofty
eminence stands proudly above the surrounding countryside. Whilst it is far from
being the highest of the Misty Mountains, much shorter in stature than the
Redhorn, Silvertine or Cloudyhead, it is nevertheless the quite the highest peak
for many leagues.
name: A Towering Summit

desc:
Climbing down from the mountain's lofty peak, this ridgeline must once have
offered a route to the foothills below. Now, however, that avenue is closed, for
the ridgeline which once travelled south from here has been shattered, leaving
behind it a mighty crater. The walls of this deep hollow are highly unstable,
and even as you view the area, another landslide sends rocks and stones
cascading into the depths, testifying to the danger of this region.
name: Above a Shattered Ridge

desc:
Multi-coloured wildflowers accent the lush green grass as the meadow flows to
the north and east. The rich earth, in which the field flourishes, is soft and
yielding to any pressure, and offers a suitable environment in which a few small
bushes and shrubs can thrive.
name: A Wild Meadow

desc:
Cutting across the countryside from east to west is an extensive mountain ridge.
Here it reaches a low point, almost petering out completely as the lands
immediately to the north and south rise to meet it. To the south lies a narrow
valley, whilst northward you can see a steady drop-off toward the southeastern
end of the Dimrill Dale, called by the dwarves Azanulbizar.
name: On a Faltering Ridge

desc:
Veering now from the north to the east, the narrow trail continues its march
beside the stream. But as it leaves the mountains behind, so the path changes in
nature. Whereas to the north and west it was somewhat rough and very rocky, the
further you follow it to the east, the more you find that the ground upon which
you travel is overgrown with dark green grass. However, here and there you can
mark the signs of use, where the grass is trampled and the earth has been
scuffed.
name: On a Turning Trail

desc:
The trail winds downward, then up again, through a bowl-like depression lined
with dark, granular earth. Despite the coarse nature of the soil the undergrowth
is lush and abundant with alpine grasses and small ground-hugging plants. The
river winds on away to the north and to your south the land smoothes into
distant grasslands.
name: At a Large Cirque

desc:
The trail twists alongside a sheer drop, a great distance above the river, with
no easy access to the sparkling river below. All about, short, sturdy trees and
hardy grasses cling tenaciously to the rocky soil, eking out whatever sustenance
they can glean from the unforgiving terrain.
name: A Sheer Rise

desc:
The watercourse beside which the trail travels has already become quite a lively
little stream. Even so close to the spring from which it arose, the merry voice
of the Silverlode can be heard clearly from the path, as the waters tumble along
their rocky bed. South then east, the trail parallels the creek, turning away
from the Misty Mountains, which tower above you to the west and to the north.
name: A Trail along a Stream

desc:
Running downwards from the dale, the path now draws close to a deep, natural
well of crystal clear water. A small rill tumbles over the stone lip of the
spring, sparkling and splashing its way along a narrow, rocky course, soon to be
augmented by numerous mountain streams, and become the mighty Silverlode. The
trail turns south from here, and traces a route parallel to the stream.
name: On a Path beside a Spring

desc:
Alive with a melody of musical murmurs and burblings, the stream gathers
strength from many tendrils of glistening water as it rushes down a steep, rocky
channel. The Silverlode leaps down the trough of the valley, running on and away
into the lower lands, slowly dissolving into a golden haze many miles distant.
name: A Gathering of Waters

desc:
The land slopes upward to the north, first gradually, then becoming a sheer
slope thrusting upwards towards great Fanuidhol, his crown wreathed in
everlasting clouds. Rough furrows carved by tiny rivulets of rainfall and
snowmelt run to the south into the swift-flowing Silverlode.
name: At the Feet of the Misty Mountains

desc:
Sculpted from the rock by a great finger of ice, this hollow is now shrouded by
great bushes of holly - a perfect hiding place for fauna seeking shelter from
the ravages of wind and cold, or perhaps hiding from unwanted attention.
Fanuidhol, mantled in grey, presides over the environs with a stark, indifferent
grandeur, blotting the northern horizon.
name: A Deep Hollow

desc:
Spread across the landscape is a great field of talus deposits; a mass of small,
perfectly smooth pebbles of basalt polished to a dark, silvery, sheen by the
weather over countless ages. Interspersed here and there are much larger chunks
of volcanic debris looming above the rocks and scree. To the south, Silverlode
plunges over falls of green-hued stone, the clear water sparkling in the light
of the skies.
name: A Glacial Moraine

desc:
Glacial debris lies piled in large mounds, tumbled from the mountains above and
fractured. Petite white blooms struggle for life among the rocks, their pale
beauty contrasting with the barren stones. Below to the south, a small building
lies nested in a wind-sheltered nook.
name: A Roughly Tumbled Slope

desc:
Rising in a great stone-toothed arch above the landscape, this craggy dark ridge
of stone seems to have been ground and polished to a fine sheen by some
indomitable force. Little to no vegetation grows on it, finding no good foothold
on its shining flanks. A trail does manage to find some tenuous line of safe
passage along it, winding slowly down, following the contours of the ridgeline.
name: Spine-backed Ridge

desc:
A small flat area opens up on the ridge above the river. It is ringed with
fir-trees, short and bent. The slopes drop steeply downwards to the north and
east, while to the west the terrain is clothed with harts-tongue and shrubs of
whortleberry. There is a small spring here, making this a good place to camp and
draw water.
name: Small Spring on a Ridge

desc:
An old cottage stands in the middle of a fluttering sea of verdant grasses
beside the glittering Silverlode. Tiny blossoms like fairy dust are spread
amidst the growing carpet. The idyllic panorama is framed by the towering
mountains to the north and west.
name: An Old Cottage by the River

desc:
Warped floorboards and mouldy discoloured walls frame a small entry into what
was was once a cosy riverside cottage. Crude pegs fashioned from sawed-off tree
branches protrude from the eastern interior above a short wooden enclosure
partially caked with mud. The western wall is extremely rotten, having seen more
than its share of moisture due to the sagging roof on that side of the abandoned
dwelling.
name: Rundown Entryway

desc:
A cold, dark fireplace sits unused in the centre of a large room that must have
been used as a primary living area. Old cobwebs now occupy every corner, though
even the inhabitants of the webs seem to have moved on. A thin layer of fine
dust has settled on the floor and atop every surface, while the northern wall
appears damp and rotten from moisture that has seeped in over time. Several
small tufts of fur ranging in colour as well as thickness lay scattered across
the floor as if left unnoticed by the previous occupant.
name: Abandoned Cottage

desc:
Various crates and miscellaneous tools still lean against the walls of an old
storeroom left in disarray. Sturdy shelves line the walls near the low ceiling,
and a pair of large hooks remain fastened securely to the eastern wall. A thin
cord, mouldy and somewhat frayed, still dangles diligently between two sets of
shelves on opposite sides of the room.
name: Disused Storeroom

desc:
A meagre nook containing only a makeshift bed overlooks the river to the south
through a crude but carefully constructed window. The alcove protrudes from the
back of the cottage as if it were added on after the rest of the dwelling had
been initially constructed. It affords more living space in the central room
while moving the bed into a separate, cosy area with a good view of the nearby
water.
name: Small Alcove

desc:
Flat lands of dun and grey are relieved by tufts and patches of ribbon-grass,
upon which bloom minute alpine berry-blossoms. To the north are the grand peaks
of the Misty Mountains. To the west are more peaks, albeit shrouded in fog and
clouds. Beckoning the true of heart, the Golden Wood can be seen stretching out
to the east.
name: Tufted Meadows

desc:
Mottled patches of heath and briar are interspersed with tufts of dark green
grasses. To the east a great forest arises; tall summits pierce the skies to the
north. A small burned patch of grass next to a boulder denotes that this place
was recently used as a camp by some passer-by.
name: Scrub and Heather

desc:
A few oak and beech trees stand widely spaced among open fields of scrub and
tender grasses. The wind can blow freely between the boughs carrying pollen and
tree scents to the insects that dwell here. To the southeast there are fewer
flowering plants and, perhaps not coincidentally, the forest also deepens.
name: Forest Borderlands

desc:
Here, a giant mulberry tree stands tall and proud, looking from afar like the
canopy of a chariot. It seems to beckon in welcome, its limbs softly stirring
even in the lightest of breezes. Floral creepers wind up its trunk, caressing
its limbs, and beyond it the grand forest quickly thickens, concealing what lies
beyond.
name: At the Threshold of Lothlorien

desc:
The trees grow in small huddles. Each stand like a small congregation, they
mutter softly amongst themselves, their limbs stirring in the breeze. The forest
grows more dense to the east, impenetrable to the eye. Thorny creepers cling to
trees, legs, and clothing in turn.
name: Huddles of Trees

desc:
The tallest peaks of the Misty Mountains loom above you to the north, grey-
spired veterans of ages long past. The days of their existence stretch back into
memories so distant as to resemble the tremulous mists that now enfold the
mountain heights. These memories are now reflected only in one place, the eaves
of Lothlorien.
name: Below the Peaks

desc:
Steely-coloured stone that has rolled and slid from the lofty peaks above give
way to softly undulating hills dotted with briar and spotty heath. A variety of
small bushes and clumps of scrub support a moderate amount of fauna, which
struggle to grow amid the wayward rocks.
name: Sloping Uplands

desc:
The rolling spaces here support a healthy quantity of vegetation, despite the
apparent absence of water. Despite the growth, the land nonetheless has a bleak
quality, cast in grey, only relieved by the sprinkling of petite white flowers
shining amidst the thorns.
name: Dun Hills

desc:
The land swells abruptly, revealing a backbone of steely grey stone, above which
cling hardy grasses and tumbled patches of gorse and brambles. Forest extends as
far as one can see to the southeast, but to the west is a wide saucer-shaped
flatland of verdant lawns.
name: Rocky Scrubland

desc:
First a lone tree, then scattered clusters of broad-leafed deciduous trees stand
side by side with pristine mellyrn, the golden trees as fresh and vibrant as the
days before Arda was sullied by the hands of Morgoth. To your east, the forest
thickens into an endless sea of mellyrn.
name: Forest Borderlands

desc:
Hundreds and hundreds of young mellyrn rise here, silver-skinned and elegant,
like a promenade of beauties at a grand, rustic ball. More mellyrn spread out in
all directions, growing fresh and unsullied, almost like they did during the age
of the lamps.
name: Fields of Mellyrn

desc:
Tall mellyrn grow profusely here, their graceful, smooth trunks splaying out
into weaving branches that shade the sky. The ground beneath is soft turf, rich
soil that supports a variety of grasses and small flowers in addition to the
grand old trees.
name: Golden Woods

desc:
One great mallorn stands here above all others, its limbs stretching far, far
above you. Dense foliage blankets its branches, and the other trees seem to
huddle close to the matriarchal tree, their tops nodding slowly in the breeze
like sleepy children huddled for a bedtime story.
name: Within the Forest

desc:
Tall and proud, ranked in evenly spaced rows, mellyrn raise their limbs to the
sky, their branches draped upon each other to form a dense canopy. Light filters
down through the spaces, glimmering and dancing upon the waters of Silverlode.
The forest continues to the east, deepening and thickening.
name: Beneath the Forest Canopy

desc:
Scattered stands of straight-limbed oaks coupled with an occasional rowan grow
here, thickening to the east and fading to the west into fields of holly and
heather. To the south and east, the forest becomes more closely knitted, and
silvery trees are seen in all directions.
name: At the Forest Border

desc:
The forest parts here and lays itself open to the arching vault of the sky. A
field of green and amber spreads out underneath the firmament, littered with
golden-eyed flowers of blue and violet. Birdsongs echo on the breeze, their
melodies complimented by the soothing murmurings of the Silverlode.
name: A Glade beside the Silverlode

desc:
Rendered craggy and twisted by the wind, these small scrub-oaks nevertheless
display a hearty vitality and leafy green verdure. Beneath them the grass grows
lush and thick in the fine silt cast aside by the Silverlode in her relentless
journey into Lorien.
name: Amidst Some Scrub-Oaks

desc:
Rising waist-high to a man, the grass here flows like water in the wind, dancing
to the weather's whim. A small sandy bank lies alongside the river, shimmering
like mother-of-pearl. Beneath the clear water a bed of rocks lies distorted by
the flowing stream.
name: A Sea of Grass

desc:
Resplendent in shades of blue and aquamarine, the Silverlode bustles merrily,
splashing, frolicking and shimmering with all the colours of the rainbow. There
is an old settlement to the north, and behind it, fields of the purest green
rise slowly from her northern banks, fading into purple mountain highlights not
so far to the north.
name: Curve in the River

desc:
Vibrant and alive with a myriad of glimmering reflections, the icy waters of
Silverlode tumble gracefully over a fall of green-hued stone, reforming in a
spray of foam amidst a beautiful dell. A sharp ridge looms above you to the
south, and to the north the dell is replete with fir-trees and layered
underneath with lushly verdant brush and undergrowth.
name: Small Waterfall

desc:
Here a bubbling stream running from the west joins its waters with the hurrying
flow of the Silverlode. Together they rush forth towards a glimmering falls, the
water pouring over the drop to a boiling foam at the foot of the cascade.
name: The Glimmering, Blue Water

desc:
Dodging between great rounded boulders, the Silverlode divides into swiftly-
flowing rivulets before reforming again and hurrying on her merry way onwards to
Lorien. A number of fir-trees, short and bent, grow on a steep ridge to the
west.
name: Flowing River

desc:
The Silverlode swells her banks, slowing her progress momentarily, her force
dissipated into swirling eddies and crosscurrents. To the north is a tall,
waving sea of grass, the opposite bank in contrast is a thin finger of sand.
name: The Silverlode

desc:
Some small trees and shrubs line Silverlode's banks; all in all this seems a
peaceful part of the river, replete with twittering birds and the gentle sighing
of the wind. The gentle crackling of clear water over a bed of rocks can be
heard as the current makes its way down stream.
name: Twisting Silverlode

desc:
With its low ceiling and bare rock walls this is not a pleasant place. A
heavy-looking oaken prison door blocks the exit into a neighbouring cell to the
north, and this cell continues further to the south. A few bundles of rugs lie
on the ground, obviously used for sleeping.
name: A Dark Cell

desc:
The red stone of the chamber walls has been carefully carved to form an almost
perfect circle - not for any aesthetic reason but a much more sadistic one - the
screams of the victims will reflect off the walls causing their shrieks to echo.
Prisoners nearby will be in no doubt as to the fate awaits them in here. It is
clear that this room has been in near-constant use for a long time. Filthy,
bloodied rags litter the floor, and the hideous devices that litter the room are
worn from frequent use. To the north-west a furnace burns, filling the room with
sulphurous fumes.
name: Chamber of Suffering

desc:
A long, low tunnel extends to the east, where it abruptly ends in a featureless
stone wall. Along the southern edge of the passage a long, low table holds
examples of the torturer's art; knives, thumbscrews, shears, pokers, and collars
of various designs, all worn and more than a few bloodied. The northern wall is
dominated by a large wooden device, its various straps and attachments folded
and gathering dust. The torturer seems to have moved on from simple physical
pain to more mental form of torment for his victims. In the corner, next to the
abandoned equipment, a wooden lever sticks out of the wall.
name: Torturer's Laboratory

desc:
The trail descends at a sharp angle from the west to the east, leading down
towards a shallow valley cut into the rock. Further ahead, a small stream begins
to run parallel to the trail. An almost vertical mountainside rises to the north
of you.
name: The Redhorn Pass

desc:
A crevasse splitting vast walls of ice to the north here broadens into a frozen
cave with smooth, ridged walls and dangling icicles. The air is warm for such
cold surroundings, and the floor is strikingly devoid of ice. Old gnawed bones
and mounds of shed fur suggest this cave is frequently inhabited.
name: Glacier Ice Cave

desc:
Warm vents in the rocky floor have carved out a large cave in the ice of a
glacier. The ceiling is smooth and rolls in long ridges perpendicular to the
walls of the cave. Steam rises from several pools of water, making the air oddly
humid in these cold surroundings. A thawing, half-eaten corpse of an elk rests
next to one of the pools. The cave climbs steeply upward to the east, where it
narrows again into a mere crack in the ice.
name: Glacier Ice Cave

desc:
The Silverlode hurries along, eager and swift. Its waters bubble and shimmer as
they wander over the streambed composed of polished stone. As the path of the
river winds its way through the landscape the water laps gently against the
riverbank.
name: The Silverlode

desc:
Roiling and gurgling through a steep gorge, constrained by cliffs of sombre hue,
the Silverlode rushes over a bed of glistening pebbles. Silvery fish shimmer
amidst playgrounds of aquamarine, holding the promise of tasty fare to the
traveller in need of sustenance.
name: The Twisting Silverlode
